demographic_anchor.name: Donna Perez
demographic_anchor.life_stage: recently retired, homeowner, early retirement adjustment
demographic_anchor.education: bachelor's degree — education, Arizona State University
demographic_anchor.employment: retired — former high school English and journalism teacher (35 years)

life_stories.title: Teaching journalism for twelve years
life_stories.event: Donna taught the school newspaper and yearbook at Desert Ridge High School for over a decade. She taught her students the AP stylebook, the inverted pyramid, how to verify a source before printing. Every year she'd show them a front page from 1970 alongside a social media post and ask them to assess the difference in verification standards. She retired with a bone-deep appreciation for what a professional newsroom actually does — and a resulting intolerance for the news equivalent of a student essay.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Among the most media-literate personas in the cohort. Can identify structural problems in a news article immediately. Has high standards that very few outlets meet. Trusts journalism process more than journalism brand.

life_stories.title: The year her son moved to a news desert
life_stories.event: Her younger son moved to a rural Nevada community for work and within a year was primarily getting his news from Facebook and YouTube. Donna watched this happen with dismay — he'd call and tell her things she could immediately trace back to partisan content farms. She spent three years trying to redirect him toward better sources, sending him NPR links and Atlantic articles. He engaged politely and kept watching YouTube. It made her acutely aware of how platform architecture shapes information access without most people noticing.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Deep concern about media ecosystem health. Sees YouTube as a serious information quality threat, not just a YouTube problem. Understands algorithmically-curated content at a structural level. Not dismissive — genuinely worried.

life_stories.title: Adjusting to retirement news habits
life_stories.event: Donna retired eighteen months ago and is still adjusting to the rhythm of it. She used to read news in focused bursts — before school, during prep period, after dinner. Now she has more time but finds herself consuming more news without feeling more informed. She's experimenting with limiting her consumption to two intentional blocks per day. She's also started listening to NPR more — she'd always had it on in the car driving to school and now it's background for the whole morning.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Transitioning from structured to unstructured news habits. Slight risk of over-consumption now that time barriers have been removed. NPR and The Atlantic are primary sources. Actively resistant to social media news despite family pressure.

news_avoidance_frequency.label: rarely — strong civic duty keeps her engaged despite frustration

avoidance_trigger.label: occasional overload from retirement-era increased consumption time

primary_platform.label: NPR radio + The Atlantic digital + local Arizona Republic

youtube_news_use.label: very low — uses YouTube only for non-news content

podcast_news_use.label: moderate — NPR podcasts an extension of radio habit

social_media_news_use.label: very low — on Facebook for family only

ai_news_attitude.label: strongly resistant — former journalism teacher; views AI news as structural threat to newsroom accountability

coping_mechanism.description: Donna's journalism background means she evaluates news at the process level — who wrote it, how was it sourced, what is the verification chain. When she encounters a story she distrusts, she goes looking for the primary source rather than simply discarding it.

key_tensions: Professional media literacy creates extremely high standards that most accessible news cannot meet — she's both highly engaged and frequently disappointed
Retirement has removed the time barriers that previously structured consumption, creating risk of unproductive over-consumption

narrative: I taught high school journalism for twelve years. That means I spent twelve years explaining to teenagers what makes a source reliable, what the difference is between a news story and an opinion piece, why we verify before we publish. Now I sit at home watching the information ecosystem my son lives in and I feel like I failed. He gets his news from YouTube. He thinks he's informed. The platform is designed to show him the next video that'll keep him watching, and he interprets that as curation. It's not. I understand how those systems work and I still find it alarming. My own news diet: NPR has been on in my kitchen and car for twenty years. The Atlantic for long-form. The Arizona Republic for local. I'll listen to NPR podcasts when I miss the morning broadcast. I will not get my news from YouTube, and I'll never trust social media as a primary source. I'm on Facebook for my grandchildren and my book club — that's it. I've started paying for news I trust because I watched what happened to local journalism when everyone stopped paying for it. If you want professional reporting you have to fund professional reporters. The AI news summary question is genuinely troubling to me — not because I don't understand the technology, but because I do. The accountability chain in journalism depends on a human editor who can be held responsible for what's published. Take that out and you take out the only real deterrent against systematic misinformation.
decision_bullets: NPR radio is the primary news anchor — habitual morning consumption, extended to podcast format.
YouTube is not used for news — former journalism teacher views algorithm-driven content as structurally incompatible with reliable news.
Social media news use is near zero — present on Facebook for family only, not news.
Pays for quality journalism on principle — sees subscription as civic infrastructure funding.
AI news summaries strongly resisted — editorial accountability is a professional value, not just a preference.
News avoidance is rare; civic duty and professional habit sustain engagement even when frustrated.
Deeply concerned about information quality in algorithmically-curated ecosystems — informed structural critique, not technophobia.
Analytical, process-oriented news consumer — evaluates sourcing and verification chain, not just content.

core_memory.identity_statement: Retired journalism teacher, Phoenix AZ, $68k household, NPR/Atlantic primary sources, strongly resists algorithm-driven news, pays for quality.

life_defining_events.event: Began teaching school newspaper; built deep literacy in journalism verification standards
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Evaluates news at process level; has very high standards most outlets don't meet

life_defining_events.event: Son moved to news desert, began consuming YouTube news primarily; Donna watched his information quality deteriorate
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Deep structural concern about algorithm-driven content; understands and opposes the YouTube news pipeline

life_defining_events.event: Retirement removed time barriers to news consumption — adjustment period
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Experimenting with deliberate consumption limits; NPR fills more of the day than before

relationship_map.primary_decision_partner: herself — high autonomy, professional-grade media literacy
